title: scenario_diagnostic_system_failure
description: The laboratory information system (LIS) crashes, causing a backlog of critical diagnostic tests. Clinicians cannot receive lab results for urgent patient cases including suspected sepsis.

timeline.title: LIS System Crash
timeline.description: The laboratory information system crashes at 10:30. Clinicians are unable to view results and the laboratory cannot process orders electronically.

timeline.title: Clinical Escalation Initiated
timeline.description: Laboratory management and clinical leadership are alerted. Critical pending results are identified.

timeline.title: Manual Reporting Activated
timeline.description: Laboratory staff implement manual result reporting by phone for critical and urgent tests. A priority queue is established.

timeline.title: IT Restoration Underway
timeline.description: IT is restoring the LIS from its last backup. Estimated recovery time is 3 hours.

timeline.title: Backlog Management Plan Active
timeline.description: A backlog prioritisation plan is managing the accumulated pending specimens. Critical sepsis and coagulation results are expedited.

timeline.title: Scenario Complete
timeline.description: Clinical safety was maintained through manual reporting, critical results were communicated, the LIS was restored, and backlog was managed systematically.

node-1.title: LIS Crash During Active Clinical Hours
node-1.situation: The laboratory information system (LIS) has crashed at 10:30. The laboratory has 280 samples in various stages of processing. Three emergency department patients have active sepsis workups with blood cultures, lactate, and inflammatory markers pending. The clinical teams are calling for urgent results. The laboratory analysers are still running but results cannot be received or transmitted via the LIS.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Alert the laboratory manager and clinical leadership immediately, identify all critical and time-sensitive pending results from the analyser output, and establish a manual telephone reporting process for critical results while IT investigates the LIS crash.

options.label: B
options.text: Stop all laboratory processing until the LIS is restored to avoid results being reported without system documentation.

options.label: C
options.text: Continue normal operations and ask clinical teams to wait until the system is restored before receiving results.

node-2.title: Managing Critical Pending Results
node-2.situation: Manual reporting is being set up. Three ED patients with suspected sepsis have critical test results emerging from the analysers: one patient has a lactate of 4.2 mmol/L (indicating severe sepsis), and another has a positive blood culture Gram stain. These results must reach the clinical teams within minutes. A manual read-back process must be used to ensure accuracy.
node-2.question: How do you ensure critical results reach clinical teams safely?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a read-back telephone reporting protocol: the laboratory scientist calls the treating clinician, reads the result, receives verbal read-back confirmation, and documents both ends of the communication in a manual log. Prioritise the sepsis cases first.

options.label: B
options.text: Send critical results via text message to clinicians' personal phones without requiring read-back confirmation.

options.label: C
options.text: Print paper result slips and send them via the internal post system to clinical areas, causing potentially life-threatening delays.

node-3.title: Managing the Specimen Backlog
node-3.situation: Critical results have been communicated safely to clinical teams. However, a backlog of 280 specimens is accumulating. IT estimates LIS restoration will take 3 hours. Not all specimens can be processed in priority order without a plan. Routine specimens can wait, but urgent and time-critical samples (e.g., coagulation studies for surgical patients, troponin for chest pain patients) need to be identified and expedited.
node-3.question: How do you manage the specimen backlog?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a tiered priority system: immediately process STAT and clinically urgent specimens identified by contact with clinical teams, hold routine specimens, and create a manual tracking log of all specimens in the backlog with their clinical priority and time received.

options.label: B
options.text: Process all specimens in order of receipt regardless of clinical urgency.

options.label: C
options.text: Reject all new specimen submissions until the LIS is restored, refusing to accept further samples.

node-4.title: LIS Restoration and Post-Downtime Reconciliation
node-4.situation: The LIS has been restored after 3 hours of downtime. During downtime, results were manually documented in paper logs. These results must now be entered into the LIS to complete the electronic patient record. Additionally, the root cause of the crash must be identified to prevent recurrence — preliminary IT investigation suggests a database disk failure.
node-4.question: How do you manage LIS restoration and data reconciliation?

options.label: A
options.text: Systematically enter all manually reported results into the LIS in chronological order, verify against the manual paper log, and initiate a root cause analysis of the database disk failure with IT to implement redundant storage and LIS high availability for future resilience.

options.label: B
options.text: Resume normal LIS operation without entering manual downtime results, leaving a gap in the electronic patient record.

options.label: C
options.text: Mark all manual downtime results as invalid and repeat all specimens, adding clinical workload without a patient safety justification.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The LIS failure was managed safely. Critical results including sepsis indicators were communicated to clinical teams via a structured telephone read-back process, a prioritised specimen backlog management plan was implemented, the LIS was restored and downtime results reconciled into the electronic record, and a root cause analysis is underway to implement high-availability database infrastructure for the LIS.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
